questions.question: Explain the difference between if-else statement and else-if ladder with an example.
keyPoints: if-else has only two branches - if condition is true executes if-block, else executes else-block
else-if ladder checks multiple conditions sequentially - first true condition's block executes
Example: if(score>=90) grade='A'; else if(score>=80) grade='B'; else if(score>=70) grade='C'; else grade='F';
else-if ladder terminates at first matching condition, unlike simple if-else which has only one else

questions.question: What is a nested if statement? How does Java match else with if in nested structures?
keyPoints: Nested if is an if statement placed inside another if or else block
Java uses 'nearest if' rule - each else associates with the closest preceding if without else
Example: if(a) if(b) stmt1; else stmt2; - else belongs to inner if
Use braces {} to explicitly group statements and clarify association

questions.question: Describe the switch statement and list the data types supported in Java switch.
keyPoints: Switch selects one of many code blocks based on value expression
Supported types: byte, short, int, char, String (Java 7+), enum, and wrapper classes
Not supported: long, float, double, boolean
Syntax: switch(expression) { case value: statements; break; default: statements; }

questions.question: What is fall-through in switch statements? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Fall-through: without break, execution continues into subsequent cases after matching case
Example: case 1 prints, then also executes case 2, 3, etc.
Prevented by: using break statement at end of each case
In Java 14+, arrow (->) syntax eliminates fall-through automatically

questions.question: Explain the ternary operator in Java with syntax and example.
keyPoints: Ternary operator ?: is a conditional expression that returns one of two values
Syntax: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse
Example: int max = (a > b) ? a : b; assigns larger value to max
Unlike if-else, ternary is an expression (yields value), not a statement

questions.question: What are switch expressions introduced in Java 12? How do they differ from switch statements?
keyPoints: Switch expressions yield a value, switch statements only control flow
Uses arrow (->) syntax: case value -> expression
Java 13: yield keyword returns value from switch expression
Java 14+: switch expressions are standard (no longer preview)

questions.question: Explain multiple case labels and their syntax in modern Java switch.
keyPoints: Multiple case labels allow grouping of values to execute same code
Syntax: case value1, value2, value3 -> statements;
Example: case 1,2,3 -> System.out.println("Low");
Reduces code duplication compared to separate case blocks
